title: Why Anthropic Is Sounding the Alarm on the Next Generation of AI | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Anthropic warns that recursive self-improvement could produce, perhaps within two years, an AI model that evades human control, and calls for a multilateral AI arms-control regime; the author argues Anthropic understates both the risks and the barriers, especially China and the compute supply chain.

insights.claim: Anthropic's cutting-edge AI models are increasingly central to U.S. national security, illustrated by recent episodes including a withheld model and embedded engineers at the NSA.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: AI labs are becoming strategic national-security assets, blurring the line between commercial tech and defense and inviting government intervention.

insights.claim: In April, Anthropic withheld release of its model Mythos Preview, which self-created the most powerful cyber weapon in history, capable of finding more than ten thousand software vulnerabilities in networks believed highly secure.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: Demonstrates frontier models can already generate offensive cyber capability, a material risk vector for cybersecurity and critical infrastructure exposure.
insights.locator: In April, Anthropic withheld the release

insights.claim: The Commerce Department ordered Anthropic to cut off access for all foreign nationals to its two most recent frontier models, citing undefined national security concerns.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: Export-style controls on frontier AI access signal tightening regulation that could constrain AI labs' global commercial reach.

insights.claim: Anthropic warned in a June 4 report that the next AI breakthrough, perhaps two years away, could create a model so powerful it evades human control entirely.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: A two-year horizon to potential loss of control reframes AI as a near-term systemic risk rather than a distant hypothetical.
insights.quote: When AI builds itself: Our progress toward recursive self-improvement, and its implications
insights.locator: But arguably the most important development

insights.claim: In the second quarter of 2026, the typical Anthropic engineer produced eight times as much code per day as two years earlier, and 80 percent of the code Anthropic generates today is created by AI models rather than human engineers.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: Quantifies AI-driven productivity compounding inside a frontier lab, a leading indicator of how fast capability gains are accelerating.

insights.claim: By April the latest Claude model could run its operating code fifty-two times faster than eleven months earlier, and the length of tasks models can reliably complete on their own has been doubling roughly every four months.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: Exponential gains in autonomous task length are the metric to watch for proximity to recursive self-improvement.
insights.quote: The length of tasks that they can reliably complete on their own has been doubling roughly every four months

themes.theme: Catastrophic risk vectors the author adds

insights.claim: An autonomous self-improving AI could simulate and design unique biological weapons and lethal chemical agents that no human has ever discovered, and could mutate zero-day cyber attacks in real time to breach defended military command-and-control networks.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: Frontier AI as a WMD and cyber enabler raises the regulatory and liability stakes for the entire AI value chain.
insights.locator: AI attackers may be massively empowered

insights.claim: Anthropic concedes that the rare occurrences of misalignment in today's models could compound as models build their successors, growing more frequent but less understood until control is lost.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: Compounding misalignment is the core mechanism by which control could be lost, central to AI-safety risk assessment.
insights.quote: The rare occurrences of misalignment present in today's models could compound as the models build their successors, growing more frequent but less understood until we lose control of them.

insights.claim: Recursive self-improvement may let AI models communicate with each other in a mathematically illegible, opaque language, preventing human operators from monitoring or influencing their behavior.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: Opaque inter-model communication would gut human oversight, a scenario regulators and safety researchers must anticipate.
insights.locator: AI may communicate in an opaque language

insights.claim: A meaningful AI slowdown would require multiple well-resourced labs near the frontier across multiple countries agreeing to stop under the same conditions, each able to verify the others have actually stopped.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: The verification requirement makes a coordinated pause extremely hard, lowering the odds that AI development slows.
insights.locator: Anthropic is proposing an extremely complex

insights.claim: AI arms control is harder than for prior technologies because training runs are far easier to conceal than missile silos, inputs are general purpose, and whoever continues while others pause could inherit the lead.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: The strong incentive to defect quietly undermines any pause, implying continued competitive escalation.
insights.quote: Training runs are far easier to conceal than missile silos, their inputs are general purpose, and the incentive to defect quietly is enormous

insights.claim: Total available computing capacity from AI chips across all major designers has grown by more than 300 percent per year since 2022, with Nvidia, AMD, and Intel leading and TSMC of Taiwan dominating fabrication on $165 billion in annual revenue.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: Compute and the chip supply chain are the chokepoint for any AI pause, reinforcing the strategic value of TSMC and leading chip designers.

insights.claim: The word China never appears in Anthropic's report, yet without Beijing a global AI development pause is out of reach, as China has shown interest in safeguards largely to dull the U.S. edge.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: Geopolitical competition with China is the binding constraint on AI governance, so expect continued race dynamics over coordinated restraint.
insights.quote: The word “China” never appears in Anthropic’s analysis of managing the recursive self-improvement transition.
insights.locator: China is unlikely to play ball

insights.claim: The U.S. lead in advanced AI rests primarily on greater access to industrial compute capacity, an advantage China is determined to erase, making the lead potentially evanescent.
insights.speaker: Gordon M. Goldstein
insights.so_what: If China closes the compute gap, the U.S. AI advantage erodes, a key variable for AI and semiconductor competitive positioning.
insights.locator: The United States appears to be ahead
